speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] 1996 and we're in Sydney, Australia. Camera is ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, you were describing to us what you saw when you went entered Belsen. Can you just tell us what the inmates looked like? Were they men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Skeletal. I think funnily enough, I didn't see many women. I think I mainly only saw men. There were some women there, but I think sometimes very hard to distinguish. You know, they're wobbling rags, literally. I must say they were piles of the dead there, piles dead, piles of half living. We went in some of the huts, all wooden huts there and some were still in their wooden beds. You know. But I suppose you could call it communal beds that they had there. They were half dead, limber half dead. [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] A very, very-- you know, you just can't believe what you saw and I thought to myself there must be hundreds of these camps around Germany which we knew about. And I think myself and this other Jewish friend of mine, David Goldstein, I think we were the only Jewish-- two Jewish soldiers there-- there that went into it with that field regiment. And I mean, I saw some English soldiers break down and cry. I cried, you know, with what we saw. I can't believe it, sat down and cried. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We gave assistance to the ones that we-- that were still alive, gave them biscuits, not in big quantities. We all knew not to feed them too much because, you know, the condition they were in. A lot of them were taken by ambulance, I think to the field hospitals were remaining there. My job, unfortunately, was bulldozing the corpses into these big pits.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who put the bodies into--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who put the bodies into the area to be bulldozed? Who carried the bodies there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you didn't. You bulldozed them which was pretty awful. I had to wear a mask over my face, the stench.

speaker: interviewer
text: If a body died in the cabin, in the huts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the ones that were alive or barely alive, they took out in field ambulance. I think they probably took them to Belsen, the town itself, put them in the hospitals. I don't know. A lot of them were sort of lifted out, taken away from the camp, you know, treatment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did the lifting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, British soldiers and Germans, Germans that were there. They were made to do it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think a lot of German civilians were brought into the camp, I recollect, brought to a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What for?

speaker: interviewee
text: To help taking out the mere-life prisoners, you know, onto ambulances and onto trucks to move them to-- by the way, there was a local hospital they just made facilities for these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could speak German.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] Did you speak to any of the Germans, the civilians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans? No. No. I didn't speak to them at all. I just saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak to any of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I looked at them with bloody hatred. I'll be quite honest, you know? No, I didn't speak to any German. I didn't want to. I didn't feel like it. And we were kept away from them, actually, you know. The command-- command officers there just didn't allow us to contact them either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak to any of the inmates that were still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I spoke to a few of them that was still laying on the ground, not inside the huts. They were lying on the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Come?

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I said to one of them who very, very frailly was covered with a plaid coat. [INAUDIBLE] a blanket and I heard him say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? And he picked up my hand and kissed me on the hand. And I-- I said to him, you'll be all right. You know. And funnily enough, I think they were all Jews in Belsen. I came across, I think, quite a few gypsies and non-Jews. They were also prisoners there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I tell you what, I don't think I could have begged to stay there longer than I did. I think I was ready to run away about two or three days. A think two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole time you were bulldozing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We doing-- we were clearing up as well. We were helping, giving assistance where we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any children, Michael?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't see any children. Can't remember seeing any children. I think mainly adults I remember seeing there. Don't remember seeing any children, unless they-- they were there and they'd been moved out by the Germans. I don't know unless they'd already been moved out prior to our get-- getting into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go inside the buildings other than the huts, the sleeping quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I only went into one or two of the huts there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sanitation facilities were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see any. I think I recollect seeing one. In one of the huts in the far corner, some sort of toilet which smelled bloody horrible. I think that toilet was for all the inmates of that particular hut. I remember that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No, I doubt think there was any sanitation there at all. I think they had to do the initial things out in the open or even on the floors of the hut, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the barracks look like, what you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were wood. They're [? wood, well, I ?] remember them. They were tiered inside. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any matting or bedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I can't-- I didn't see any. I didn't see any at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw straw, a lot of straw there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about food? Was there any food being supplied at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. By the British Army, and the-- was preparing food better, and I think they were very careful the way they supplied it. You know. We were told not to over feed them. You know, to look after them.

speaker: interviewer
text: In any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- then it wasn't my job in any case. That was a-- I think the-- what was it, K3 Corps? Can't remember. But food was brought into the camp, you know, to feed them soup or things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any remains of cooking or kitchen facilities that they may have had there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't recollect, Susie. I can't recollect. It's gonna be too far back. It's only certain things that stand in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see--

speaker: interviewee
text: I probably didn't attach any importance to that kind of thing at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see a gas chamber or a torture chamber at all? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we didn't see it. I didn't see it. I think there was some ovens there. I think there was because I remembered before we entered the camp, there was a lot of smoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you could smell something. What could you smell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well I smelled dead bodies and stench and I mean, the place smelled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any animal pens there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't see any.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any dogs left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any hospital facilities at all--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see any.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or medical facility?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see any.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any writings on buildings at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think the numbers on the huts, but that's about all I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No names indicating buildings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't-- can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Germans apart from the ones that you mentioned still there, soldiers captured? Were there any commanders still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only one I remember seeing was Josef Kramer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had his function been?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a camp commandant there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned Irma Grese.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Irma Grese came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her function?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in charge of the women's quarters, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her reaction of the living inmates to these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think they had any strength in them to show any reaction. I think they were just pleased they were liberated, freed. You know. I don't think they had the strength to show any-- anything, any emotion. You know. I would say they were more like zombies. What-- what reaction could a zombie give?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were involved with the burial detail?

speaker: interviewee
text: The burial? No, I was just using the bulldozer to bulldoze these bodies that were lying around into the pits.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were-- they did become graves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, mass graves. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the Jews separated from the gypsies or was everyone buried together?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? 1,000, ?] they were all buried together. That's something I-- I just can't remember. How do you distinguish a Jew from a gypsy in a concentration camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm wondering whether there was any markings on graves, crosses at all maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, just mass grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just mass grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just big pits.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing indicating that there was a grave there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think it was probably all mixed up. [INAUDIBLE] I think, you know, on hindsight I think Belsen was mainly a contraction camp for Jewish people, Jewish inmates, but we were told no, that they were other than Jewish inmates in that camp. So with that being moved from other camps prior to capturing Belsen, I don't know, which is quite possible. You know Germans probably shifted other inmates into there, but I always had the thought that Belsen was mainly a Jewish concentration camp, but I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, where were you living at this stage? Were you in barracks or camp tents or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were in camps and I think for one day we were in a-- in a hotel building in Belsen itself, not in the camp, but the little town of Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet other people from the town of Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they'd all been evacuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't meet anybody that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think I met probably two Germans, but I don't know who they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did they know about Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure they did. I'm sure they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because apparently after I left Belsen, I was told that they got the whole German population what was left into the camps to see what they had done. And I think even the mayor of Belsen, the Bergermeister, but that was after-- but we heard that afterwards, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear their reaction was?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't believe it. They couldn't believe that Nazis, the German party could do anything like that. But you know, who knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel when you heard that that was their reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bull. You know what I mean by bull? Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what nationalities were in the Belsen camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, obviously, they were German Jews. I think they was quite also from Holland and also I think from Germany itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, you said that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many Jewish dead there were? Were you given a figure?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I could tell you. Five, three-- probably about 23,000, I think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know one-- one grave held 5,000 and another one held 3,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did someone count the bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even know. I don't even know, Susie. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, were there any rabbis there at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. There was a Jewish chaplain. I think there was a Jewish army chaplain there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I'm pretty certain I remember seeing a Jewish army chaplain there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- we were only there for three days, I believe, three or four-- I think three days at most, then we moved on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who continued your-- your work if you left? If your unit left, who continued--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I suppose another field regiment might have come in. Another branch of British Army might have come in. I don't know. But I think it took about 10 days to clean and clear the camp. I think was put to torch the day I left with flame throwers. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I think it was one or two months after the war finished even. That I'm not certain about.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you heard-- you knew what happened to Belsen after-- after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We were told it was put to torch, cleaned up, and made a monument of the place. I know that, yeah, made a memorial to the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the evacuation. Was there any evacuation going on while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, evacuation of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Civilian-- of inmates.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the inmates? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they being evacuated to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, could have been just being evacuated to Belsen itself-- another town itself or some other place I-- you know, that was already captured in Germany. You know. I don't think they could have been very, very far because they were too ill-- too weak, too ailing. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew that there was an evacuation happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Oh, yeah. I think that was the purpose of the-- you know, but I'm sure of it. You know, I mean, I saw quite a number of them taken by stretcher onto the ambulances that were there and the trucks that were there. You know. And where they were going to, I'm not quite certain, but they must be taken to a place where they could be suckled, saved, fed, clothed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction throughout all of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horror. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I shouldn't really say this, but I said to myself, there's no damned God. I became an atheist after that. I just didn't want to believe there was a God. I couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you discuss this with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you discuss this with your fellow soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, especially my friend, David Goldstein. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He felt the same way as me. [INAUDIBLE] some of the British soldiers me, some of my friends in the regiment with me. They sat down and cried like I did when we first saw it. You know, they-- they weren't Jewish and when they say it, they say down and cried. You know. They didn't cry, they wept. If they didn't wept, they sobbed. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: About the time you get over dehumanize, you see something like that. By that I mean, you know it's there, you know it's happened, and yet it's a lot disbelief. How could it happen? You know, how could anyone do anything like this? What's your worst memory of that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your worst memory of that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bulldozing the corpses into the graves. That's something I'll never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what it looked like it?

speaker: interviewee
text: What could I say? Just mass of bodies. You know, you're pushing dead bodies with a big blade into these mass graves. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the bodies clothed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the bodies clothed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, most of them were naked. Some had tatters, you know, dressed in tatters, but I suppose they died on the spot. They used to drop dead in the camp. They just didn't take any notice of them. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you're a Jewish soldier, were you taken-- your emotions taken into consideration? Did you ever say you didn't want to do this job, maybe someone else should?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to do it. As a matter of fact, after one day, I said to my [INAUDIBLE], I said, I just don't want to carry on doing this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your commander say?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I understand. He said, I understand. We had to take relays. You just couldn't do the whole time. It's just, [INAUDIBLE] nauseating. It's just you became-- you became dehumanized to think that you could-- you know, that I was shoveling Jewish bodies into a grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think of them as Jewish bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Oh, yeah. Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you angry?

speaker: interviewee
text: More than angry. I was-- I was angry with God. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Are you still angry with God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I only know I'm Jewish because I'm Jewish, but as far as him up there, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you angry with the Germans too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I was. Of course, I was. How else could you feel? You know, you think, how could people-- you know, how could they do a thing like that? You know? Especially also knowing, I suppose, because you know it wasn't only Jews. It some of their own people, you know, they did it to, lots of German, non-Jewish people were put into these camps as well just because they didn't side with the Nazi philosophy, you know, or they were socialists or they were communists or they were gypsies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't only Jews. I mean, it's just-- you know, it's just so terrible, you know. Did you hear of any atrocities that occurred apart from the actual killing of Jews? Did you hear of any experiments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where, in Germany?

speaker: interviewer
text: Throughout your war experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I think we heard quite a bit. But I think a lot of it did happen, not-- maybe not necessarily in Germany, but in places like Czechoslovakia and France where they had massacres-- massacred. There was a place called Oradour-sur-Glane where the Germans [INAUDIBLE] executed a whole village and also like in Czechoslovakia in Lidice where they slaughtered a whole village, so you heard about these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear about experiments that were going on in concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: After. After.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet soldiers of other Allied forces?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I met American soldiers, and I met Polish soldiers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't see many French soldiers. They were mainly Maquis-- the Maquis. Yeah, I met Polish soldiers. I met American soldiers and then French soldiers I didn't meet, [LAUGHS] very strange.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet any Allied prisoners of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Allied prison of war. No, I don't recollect. I don't recollect. Anything I recollect, they weren't Allied, but German slave workers who were being shipped back to their own countries, I saw that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What countries had they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I don't know, but I should think like France, Germany-- France, Poland, Russia. They love Russian slave workers that we knew about, Polish slave workers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, let's go back to 1945, May, when you left Belsen. Where did you go to after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I went to-- we went on to Magdeburg-- was it Magdeburg? Braunschweig, Hannover.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: What I can't remember, be quiet, Susie. I can't remember if I went to Belsen from Hannover or I went from Brunswick, that I can't remember, but it's either those two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's just go to Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with the occupation forces in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the British Army occupation around there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your function there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Policing, I suppose, You could call it policing. Or--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the actual function? What does that mean, policing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Keeping the population, you know, under control. Repairing damaged buildings that were being used by the British. And policing occupation army. It was called British Army Occupation of the Rhine.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a tissue.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's all right. It's just known as B-- BAOR.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin? From 1945 right till my discharge, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet displaced persons at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved with the displaced persons camps at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not at all. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved at all or did you hear about any Jewish organizations, Jewish agencies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Funnily enough in Hannover, a Jewish hospitality committee opened a canteen there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was it run by?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish-- it was called the Jewish Hospitality Committee. Now, I don't know if it was English. I don't know if it was German or what, but they opened up this canteen in Hannover. That's something I'll never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my first taste of lokshen soup there. Yeah. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet people who had survived the Holocaust concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: What, Jewish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not whilst I was in Berlin. Not whilst I was in Berlin, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you shipped back to England?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there, by ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We went by train. I went from Berlin to Hameln, which is called commonly Hamelin where the piped piper came from. From there, I went by train because all the railways were all disrupted there, so you had to go through-- go in certain lines to get to your destination.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get across to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: From England, we went by boat, naturally, but from Berlin we've been to a place called Hameln-- [INAUDIBLE]. And we went on from there to Tournai, which was in Belgium, and from there we got on a boat to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first thing you did when you reached British soil?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to bed with my wife. [LAUGHS] No. I'm joking, Don't put that in, David. Cut that out. What did I do? Well, number one, we went to a disembarkation station where we handed in our uniforms and we were given civilian clothes by the-- by the British government, which I think is the only country in the world that did. Gave you a suit, shirt, shoes, tie, and a hat, a trilby hat. And you had to hand in your uniform. Because after that, I still had five years to do in the army as a reservist. But anyway I was never called up after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went home by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, wait. Where did I land? I went to Tournai to Southampton, from Southampton, by train to London. Most of that I can't remember. I think it was Waterloo Station. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your wife meet you at Waterloo Station?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She had two kids. No, I told her I was coming home anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'd been in contact with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: By letter or by phone?

speaker: interviewee
text: By letter when I got to England. We didn't have a phone then, so that's not true. No, we couldn't afford a phone then. No, by letter, I think mainly. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were discharged in 1946.

speaker: interviewee
text: '46, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you got home, apart from the obvious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had seven days leave. I-- you know, they gave me leave from the army. I got a-- I think it was seven days. When I took my army book there. I think I had seven to 14 days leave, which I spent with my wife and my two kids. My youngest child hadn't been born yet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was suppose we're all happy war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were things tough in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Well, she went through the whole blitz, my wife did. Got bombed out twice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Food was short but not scarce. You know, they gave you a sufficient ration to be able to live on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: When? After I came? Well, I had seven days' leave then I went to work-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] this-- this I'm gonna have to remember now. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I did do some work. I don't know. I did jobs as a radio mechanic, I think, a firm I used to work for before the war. That's right, yeah. I was fixing up aerials. Mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do for the rest of your time in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started up my own business after time. I went into business on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: In electronics?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And well, I buy and sell government surplus material, you know, right up to the time I came here to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: What made me come to Australia? Well, my-- my eldest son, David, who was about 17 at the time-- this was 19-- decided to come to Australia with a friend. He wanted to try his luck here. I think that was 19-- 1967. Yeah, I think it was about 1965, he came to Australia. And then we got a letter from him to say he'd met a Jewish girl, and he was gonna get married. So my wife and I decided to come here for his wedding with my young son, Barry. Came here for the wedding. Stayed here a think for about three or four weeks. And I think we fell in love with Australia and decided to come back, so it took me a couple of years, you know, to sell the business I had there, and we came back here in 1969 and right up to the present day. Unfortunately, my wife had died in '91-- '91, yeah. But life has got to go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, from the things that you experienced during the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you've told us how it affected your religious views. How has it affected your attitudes generally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I still light Shabbos candles. I still pray to God. Right? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I feel, I still feel quite to the present day I can't believe there's a God.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your attitudes to life, generally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Life is wonderful if you're fit and well and healthy and rich. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever tell your family what you saw, what you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I mean, obviously, they've seen photos, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but they know that-- that I-- you know, I was in the army for that time. So obviously, they know I had to experience something which--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks, Michael. We'll continue in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape three of the interview with Michael Sheridan. It's the 15th of April, 1996, and we're in Sydney, Australia.
